literal: of the five nights; from pañca (five) + rātra (night) — traditional etymologies vary
invariant_definition: Pāñcarātra is an early Vaiṣṇava theological and ritual tradition (āgama), centered on the worship of Viṣṇu / Vāsudeva-Nārāyaṇa and known for its doctrine of divine emanation. Its signature teaching is the vyūha scheme: the supreme Lord manifests in a series of ordered forms (Vāsudeva, Saṃkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha) through which creation, sustenance, and grace proceed, together with the doctrine of avatāra and of the deity's accessibility in consecrated images (arcā). Its temple liturgy, initiation, and theology were absorbed into Śrīvaiṣṇavism and gave Rāmānuja's Viśiṣṭādvaita much of its devotional and ritual framework; the school also defended the scriptural validity of the Āgamas against Mīmāṃsaka and Vedāntic critics.
